body_txt: Pocket watches revolutionized high society in the 1600s, becoming the first accurate way of telling time other than the sun. In 1735, English inventor John Harrison revolutionized sea travel with the marine chronometer, a clock so accurate it would keep perfect time even on board a ship after a long voyage. This solved the Longitude Problem and made accurate marine navigation possible. But open a watch and what do you see? Just a bunch of gears and springs and other bits of metal. How do you put those together and get a watch? Then in 1802, the English clergyman William Paley argued for the existence of God with his "Watchmaker Argument:" as the mechanical perfection of the watch implied a human watchmaker, the perfection of the world implied a supernatural Watchmaker. Perhaps because of Paley's argument, perhaps because of others, characters who fix watches and clocks for a living are often portrayed as somewhat special or even having a supernatural degree of knowledge about the universe. Perhaps because of the somewhat detached nature the metaphor implies, such characters are rarely unambiguously good. Compare with The Chessmaster . See Clocks of Control for another way that clocks can be used to characterize someone.
